incidents.victim: Ukrainian prosecutors, NATO allied military (Romania, Greece, Bulgaria, Serbia)
incidents.vector: Spearphishing and credential theft harvested at least 284 email inboxes (including 170+ Ukrainian prosecutors and 67 Romanian Air Force accounts) over several months; the hackers accidentally exposed their own server, revealing the operation.
incidents.impact: Stolen emails from Ukraine's Specialized Anti-Corruption Prosecutor's Office, ARMA, and defense prosecutors, plus NATO airbase and Greek military inboxes, giving Moscow visibility into Ukrainian counterintelligence work and allied military operations.

incidents.vector: Ransomware affiliate ecosystem (notably SafePay and Qilin) exploited the weaker security posture of mid-market German firms, with SafePay claiming 76 German companies in 2025.
incidents.impact: Data-leak-site postings against German victims grew 92% year-over-year in 2025 — three times the European average — with legal and professional services firms increasingly targeted as a route to downstream client data.

incidents.vector: Zero-day spoofing vulnerability (CVE-2026-32201) in Microsoft Office SharePoint allows unauthenticated attackers to present falsified content within trusted SharePoint sites over the network.
incidents.impact: Active exploitation confirmed by CISA (added to KEV) in Microsoft's second-largest Patch Tuesday on record (165 vulnerabilities); attackers can use it to seed phishing and social engineering campaigns from inside trusted environments.

incidents.vector: Intrusion into the city attorney's IT environment enabled bulk exfiltration of case files and internal records.
incidents.impact: 7.7 TB of data published online including sensitive case details, LAPD officer personal information, witness names, medical records, and internal affairs investigations.

incidents.vector: Business email compromise: attackers tricked staff at a US subsidiary into redirecting a contractor payment.
incidents.impact: €700,000 stolen from the company's US subsidiary.

incidents.victim: ChipSoft (HiX EHR platform — ~70% of Dutch hospitals)
incidents.vector: Ransomware attack on ChipSoft, the software provider whose HiX electronic patient record system is used by roughly 70% of Dutch hospitals.
incidents.impact: Platform availability was not disrupted, but the incident creates systemic risk across the Dutch healthcare sector given the provider's dominance in hospital EHR.

incidents.vector: An unidentified group calling itself FlamingChina accessed and exfiltrated petabytes of data from China's NSCC; investigators needed weeks to authenticate the trove due to its size.
incidents.impact: Leaked files include documents marked 'secret' and appear to contain Chinese military and government data — a rare outward-facing breach of sensitive Chinese state infrastructure.

incidents.victim: Global SOHO router users (200+ organizations, 5,000+ devices, 18,000+ networks)
incidents.industry: Government, law enforcement, email providers, critical infrastructure
incidents.vector: GRU Unit 26165 exploited known flaws in end-of-life Mikrotik and TP-Link routers to change DNS settings to GRU-controlled servers, then ran adversary-in-the-middle attacks against Microsoft Outlook Web Access to siphon OAuth tokens — no malware required.
incidents.impact: Silent harvesting of authentication tokens from ministries of foreign affairs, law enforcement, and third-party email providers worldwide; US DOJ and FBI executed court-authorized 'Operation Masquerade' to remediate US routers across 23+ states.

incidents.vector: Pro-Iranian hacktivist group launched distributed denial-of-service attacks against consumer-facing web services.
incidents.impact: Pinterest was taken offline for several hours; Chime Financial's website was also disrupted. Limited user-facing impact but public signalling amid US-Iran conflict.

incidents.vector: Successful phishing attack gave attackers access to internal systems and client files.
incidents.impact: Client materials were posted online; Jones Day (2,400+ lawyers, counsel to Trump 2016/2020 campaigns) confirmed the breach.

incidents.victim: University of Mississippi Medical Center, Passaic County NJ, plus victims in AU/UK/US healthcare, education, finance
incidents.vector: Medusa ransomware operation (Russia-based) exploits vulnerable web-facing systems — often within a week of disclosure — and moves from initial access to exfiltration and ransomware deployment in under 24 hours using tools like ScreenConnect, AnyDesk, and SimpleHelp.
incidents.impact: Devastating attack on Mississippi's largest hospital (UMMC reopened March 2 with FBI/DHS support), disruption to Passaic County NJ phones, and ongoing high-velocity targeting of healthcare and local government worldwide.

incidents.vector: Unauthenticated zero-day (CVE-2026-35616, CVSS 9.1/9.8) in FortiClient EMS exploited in the wild starting March 31, leveraging a holiday weekend window before a full patch was available.
incidents.impact: Near-2,000 publicly exposed FortiClient EMS instances at risk; US CISA and Singapore CSA issued urgent patch-within-days directives. Second serious FortiClient EMS flaw disclosed in three weeks.

incidents.vector: Qilin ransomware intrusion into the party's IT network at the end of March.
incidents.impact: Employee records stolen; party says member data was not compromised. IT network taken offline for remediation.

incidents.victim: Minot, North Dakota water treatment plant
incidents.vector: Ransomware encrypted IT systems at a municipal water treatment facility.
incidents.impact: Some IT systems encrypted, but the plant remained operational. Another illustration of ransomware pressure on US municipal utilities.

incidents.victim: Government entities across Southeast Asia (video conferencing via TrueConf)
incidents.vector: Chinese operators exploited CVE-2026-3502 in TrueConf video conferencing software (Operation TrueChaos). After compromising an on-premises TrueConf server inside a government IT department, they distributed weaponized updates through the trusted update channel to dozens of connected government entities, then deployed Havoc and ShadowPad.
incidents.impact: Dozens of Southeast Asian government bodies received malicious TrueConf updates; CISA ordered US federal agencies to patch by April 16.

incidents.vector: Initial access via the Trivy supply-chain compromise (attributed to TeamPCP) exposed a secret Amazon API key; attackers accessed the Commission's AWS account and obtained management rights that could have enabled lateral movement.
incidents.impact: Approximately 92 GB of compressed data tied to 42 internal clients and 29+ EU entities (names, email addresses, some email content) stolen on March 19; ShinyHunters posted the data on the dark web March 28.

incidents.victim: Massachusetts towns (Pepperell, Ashby, Dunstable, Groton, Townsend)
incidents.vector: Cyberattack on the Patriot Regional Emergency Communications Center (the regional 911/dispatch hub) that began April 1 disrupted IT and public-safety systems across multiple member towns.
incidents.impact: Non-emergency and business phone lines knocked offline while 911 remained operational. Scope of data exfiltration under investigation; the center is linked to CodeRED, which itself was hit by ransomware in November.

incidents.vector: Cyberattack disrupted IT networks and public services at the county government.
incidents.impact: Governor Tim Walz deployed the Minnesota National Guard cyber unit to assist. Second cyberattack against Winona County (separate from a January 2026 ransomware incident).

incidents.vector: Social engineering attack against the telehealth provider's customer support system in February.
incidents.impact: Customer names and email addresses were stolen; some personal data shared in support tickets potentially exposed.

incidents.vector: DPRK operators built a fake professional trading company, met Drift staff at a major crypto conference, deposited $1M of their own money to establish trust, then compromised employee devices via malicious Apple TestFlight apps and VS Code extensions and used the access to multi-sign treasury transactions.
incidents.impact: Approximately $280-285M in crypto-assets drained from Drift's treasury, one of the largest DeFi thefts of 2026 to date.

incidents.vector: Unspecified cyberattack over the weekend of March 28-29 prompted the toymaker to shut down affected systems and deploy intermediary infrastructure.
incidents.impact: Hasbro disclosed in an SEC 8-K filing that recovery will take several weeks; order taking and shipping continued on backup infrastructure. No ransomware group has taken credit.

incidents.victim: Axios npm library users (OpenAI, broad JavaScript ecosystem)
incidents.vector: DPRK-linked actors social-engineered an Axios project admin to compromise his credentials, then published malicious versions (v1.14.1 and v0.30.4) of the Axios HTTP client to npm. The packages introduced a fake 'plain-crypto-js' post-install dependency that fetched a platform-specific RAT.
incidents.impact: Axios sees ~100 million downloads per week; malicious versions were live for ~3 hours. Downstream victims include OpenAI (Mac apps need updates) and any developer who installed during the window. Credentials on affected systems must be rotated.

incidents.victim: LiteLLM, Checkmarx, Trivy, Cisco, Mercor, BuddyBoss and downstream users
incidents.vector: TeamPCP used a single stolen credential to gain write access to trusted repositories, then injected credential-harvesting payloads into LiteLLM (97M monthly downloads), poisoned Checkmarx GitHub Actions workflows, and cascaded through Trivy, Docker Hub, VS Code, and PyPI packages in under a week.
incidents.impact: Hundreds of thousands of AI API keys, cloud credentials, and Kubernetes tokens harvested. Cisco source code and AWS credentials reportedly stolen; European Commission, Mercor, and others confirmed downstream breaches. Telegram channel indicates monetization (ransomware, extortion of 300+ GB of data) is still unfolding.

incidents.vector: Ransomware attack on Italy's flagship art museum in February; attackers attempted extortion.
incidents.impact: Museum relocated its most valuable works (including pieces from the Medici collection and da Vinci, Raphael, and Botticelli) to secure bank vaults; Uffizi said no passwords or security maps were stolen and the incident is 'nothing like the Louvre.'
